questions.question: Write the BFS algorithm. Trace BFS on a given graph starting from a specified vertex, showing the queue state at each step.
keyPoints: BFS algorithm with queue and visited array
Initialize: mark source visited, enqueue source
Loop: dequeue, visit all unvisited neighbors, enqueue them
Step-by-step trace showing queue contents after each operation
Final BFS traversal order

questions.question: Explain how BFS finds the shortest path in an unweighted graph. Illustrate with an example.
keyPoints: BFS explores level by level (distance 0, 1, 2, ...)
First discovery of a vertex is via shortest path
Use parent/predecessor array to reconstruct path
Example showing shortest path from source to a target vertex
Why DFS cannot guarantee shortest path

questions.question: Write a C program to perform BFS traversal on a graph represented using an adjacency list.
keyPoints: Queue implementation (array-based or linked list)
Graph structure with adjacency list
BFS function with visited array and queue operations
Handling disconnected graphs by calling BFS from each unvisited vertex
Output the BFS traversal order

questions.question: Compare BFS and DFS traversal algorithms in detail with examples.
keyPoints: BFS: queue, level-order, shortest path in unweighted graphs
DFS: stack/recursion, deep exploration, cycle detection
Both have O(V+E) time complexity with adjacency list
BFS space can be larger (wide graphs), DFS space can be larger (deep graphs)
Example graph with BFS and DFS orders compared

questions.question: Explain how BFS can be used to check if a graph is bipartite.
keyPoints: Assign two colors alternately during BFS
Source gets color 0, its neighbors get color 1, their neighbors get color 0
If an already-colored neighbor has the same color as current vertex, graph is not bipartite
If BFS completes without conflict, graph is bipartite
